title: Resource Management: Food
examTip: For food questions, know both large-scale (Green Revolution, GM crops, Almería greenhouses) AND sustainable (agroforestry, Fairtrade, reducing waste) approaches — and evaluate each. The 9-mark question often asks you to compare them.
introTips: Almería is the large-scale food production case study — 30,000 hectares of greenhouses in southern Spain.
For sustainable approaches, know at least three specific examples with place names.
The Green Revolution is a two-sided story — food for billions, but also inequality and environmental costs.
intro: There is enough food produced in the world today to feed every single person — yet nearly 700 million people go hungry. The problem is not global food supply; it's who can grow it, who can afford it, and whether the systems we use to produce it are sustainable. Understanding why food insecurity exists, how we've tried to boost production, and whether those approaches are really working — that's what this lesson is about.

keyPoints.heading: Food security: patterns and causes
keyPoints.content: The world produces enough calories to feed everyone — but food is distributed very unequally. Average daily calorie intake ranges from around 3,800 kcal in the USA to 3,400 in the UK, to around 2,300 kcal in sub-Saharan Africa (the global minimum recommended is 2,100 kcal). Around 690 million people are chronically hungry. Simultaneously, about 2 billion people are overweight or obese — mainly in HICs and rapidly urbanising NEEs. This 'double burden' can exist in the same country (e.g., Brazil). Global food prices affect food security significantly — they are closely linked to fossil fuel prices (agriculture depends on fuel for transport and chemical fertilisers derived from oil), and to speculative trading of food commodities on international markets (financial investors trading food futures can drive up prices).
writeBullets: USA ~3,800 kcal/day; UK ~3,400; sub-Saharan Africa ~2,300 (minimum recommended: 2,100)
690m chronically hungry — mostly sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia
2bn overweight/obese — HICs and urbanising NEEs; double burden of malnutrition
Both under- and over-nutrition can coexist in the same country (e.g., Brazil)
Food prices linked to fossil fuel prices — transport, fertilisers, irrigation
Commodity speculation — financial trading on food markets drives up prices
keyPoints.memTip: Too much here, too little there — food insecurity is about distribution and access, not total supply.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The link between food prices and fossil fuel prices is a less obvious but important point — it shows food and energy are interconnected.

keyPoints.heading: Causes of food insecurity
keyPoints.content: Food insecurity has both physical and human causes. Physical causes: drought (the Sahel region; Somalia — the 2011 famine; the 2022 Horn of Africa drought); flooding (Bangladesh and Mozambique — destroys crops); soil degradation (erosion by wind and rain, salinisation from over-irrigation, loss of organic matter from intensive farming); pests and disease (the 2020 East Africa locust swarms destroyed crops across 10 countries). Climate change is altering growing seasons, increasing frequency of extreme weather events, and shifting rainfall patterns. Human causes: poverty (people cannot afford food even if it is available); conflict (South Sudan, Yemen, DR Congo — war disrupts farming, destroys infrastructure, forces people to flee); poor governance (aid diverted by corrupt officials); population growth (demand outstrips supply in some regions).
writeBullets: Drought — Sahel, Somalia 2011 famine, Horn of Africa 2022
Flooding — Bangladesh, Mozambique — crop destruction
Soil degradation — erosion, salinisation from over-irrigation, loss of organic matter
Pests — East Africa locust swarms 2020, destroyed crops in 10 countries
Climate change — shifting seasons, more extreme weather, unpredictable rainfall
Poverty — cannot afford food even when available
Conflict — South Sudan, Yemen, DR Congo — farming disrupted, people displaced
keyPoints.memTip: DFSPC — Drought, Flood, Soil, Pests, Conflict. Five physical/human causes of food insecurity.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often give only physical causes — always balance with human causes (poverty, conflict, governance) for higher marks.

keyPoints.heading: Increasing food production — large-scale approaches
keyPoints.content: The Green Revolution (1960s–70s) dramatically increased food production through high-yield variety (HYV) seeds, chemical fertilisers and irrigation — transforming rice and wheat yields in Asia and Latin America. Norman Borlaug's work is estimated to have saved over 1 billion people from starvation. However, the Green Revolution also increased inequality (wealthy farmers could afford inputs; small farmers could not), depleted groundwater (aquifers), and caused chemical runoff pollution. GM crops (genetically modified organisms) are another large-scale approach: herbicide-resistant soya, 'Golden Rice' (with beta-carotene to tackle Vitamin A deficiency), and drought-resistant maize. Controversy surrounds GM: the EU bans most GM crops, critics worry about corporate control of seeds and biodiversity loss. Case study: Almería, southern Spain — over 30,000 hectares of greenhouse agriculture ('sea of plastic' visible from space) produces 3.5 million tonnes of fruit and vegetables per year, exported to northern Europe. Year-round production regardless of weather — but concerns include high water use, migrant labour conditions, and soil pollution.
writeBullets: Green Revolution (1960s–70s) — HYV seeds, fertilisers, irrigation; saved 1bn+ from starvation
Green Revolution criticisms — increased inequality, aquifer depletion, chemical runoff
GM crops — herbicide-resistant soya, Golden Rice, drought-resistant maize
GM criticism — EU bans, corporate control of seeds, biodiversity concerns
Almería, Spain — 30,000+ hectares of greenhouses; 3.5m tonnes vegetables/year
Almería benefits — year-round production, exports to N. Europe, employment
Almería issues — high water use, migrant labour conditions, 'sea of plastic'
keyPoints.memTip: Green Revolution fed billions — but at the cost of inequality, water and soil. Big gains, big trade-offs.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Almería is the named case study for large-scale food production — learn the figures (30,000 ha, 3.5m tonnes) and the criticisms.

keyPoints.heading: Sustainable approaches to food
keyPoints.content: Sustainable food production aims to meet today's needs without compromising future food security or the environment. Agroforestry — growing trees alongside crops — provides shade, fixes nitrogen (reduces fertiliser need), prevents soil erosion, and creates additional income from fruit and timber (widely used in West Africa). Organic farming uses no synthetic chemicals — better for biodiversity and soil health, but typically produces lower yields and commands higher prices. Urban farming and allotments — growing food in cities reduces food miles and builds community. Reducing food waste: approximately 30% of all food produced globally is wasted — apps like Too Good To Go, supermarket date label reform, and household behaviour change can all help. Eating less meat: beef requires 15,000 litres of water per kg and vast areas of land — reducing meat consumption is one of the highest-impact individual actions. Seasonal food: avoiding air-freighted out-of-season imports dramatically reduces carbon emissions. Fairtrade: ensures farmers receive a fair price, supporting food system sustainability in LICs.
writeBullets: Agroforestry — trees + crops; reduces erosion, nitrogen-fixing, income diversification
Organic farming — no synthetic chemicals; better biodiversity but lower yields
Urban farming and allotments — reduces food miles, community benefits
Food waste reduction — 30% of global food wasted; Too Good To Go, date label reform
Eat less meat — beef: 15,000 litres water per kg; huge land use
Seasonal food — avoids air-freighted imports; major carbon reduction
Fairtrade — fair price for farmers in LICs; supports sustainable livelihoods
keyPoints.memTip: AOUF-ESF — Agroforestry, Organic, Urban, Food waste, Eating less meat, Seasonal, Fairtrade.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Sustainable approaches questions need real examples — not just concepts. Agroforestry in West Africa, Fairtrade certification, Too Good To Go are all specific enough.

keyPoints.heading: UK food security
keyPoints.content: The UK produces approximately 60% of its food needs — self-sufficient in many grains, dairy, and poultry, but imports a significant proportion of fruit and vegetables (around 45% of vegetables consumed are imported). UK food security faces several risks. Brexit: leaving the EU Single Market in 2020 introduced new trade barriers and disrupted 'just in time' supply chains for fresh produce from EU countries. Climate change: UK crop yields may increase in the short term as temperatures rise, but extreme weather events pose long-term risks. Fertiliser dependence: Russia-Ukraine war (2022) disrupted global fertiliser supply (Russia exports large quantities of nitrogen and potash fertilisers), affecting UK farming costs. The UK government published a Food Strategy in 2022 addressing supply chain resilience. Local food systems (farmers' markets, veg box schemes, community supported agriculture) are growing in popularity but remain a niche market compared to supermarket supply chains.
writeBullets: UK produces ~60% of food needs; 45% of vegetables imported
Brexit — new trade barriers; 'just in time' supply chains disrupted
Climate change — short-term yield gains likely; extreme weather a long-term risk
Russia-Ukraine war — disrupted fertiliser supply; raised UK farming costs
UK Food Strategy 2022 — government response to supply chain vulnerability
Local food systems — farmers' markets, box schemes, CSA — growing but niche
keyPoints.memTip: UK = 60% self-sufficient — good, but not secure. Brexit and war exposed the gaps.
keyPoints.tutor_note: UK food security is a frequent exam question — the 60% figure, 45% vegetable imports, and Brexit disruption are the key facts.

tables.title: Large-scale vs sustainable food production — comparison
rows: Green Revolution (large-scale)
Massively increased yields; saved 1bn+ from starvation; affordable food
Increased inequality; groundwater depletion; chemical pollution
HYV rice in India and SE Asia, 1960s–70s
GM crops (large-scale)
Drought-resistant, pest-resistant; potential to reduce chemical use
EU bans; corporate control; biodiversity concerns; public distrust
Herbicide-resistant soya (USA, Brazil)
Almería greenhouses (large-scale)
Year-round production; 3.5m tonnes/year; reliable supply to Europe
High water use; migrant labour issues; 'sea of plastic'; soil pollution
Almería, SE Spain — 30,000+ ha
Agroforestry (sustainable)
Prevents erosion, nitrogen-fixing, income diversification, biodiversity
Lower crop yields per hectare; slower to establish
West Africa — trees + crops integrated
Fairtrade (sustainable)
Better income for farmers; Fairtrade Premium for community projects
Does money reach farmers? Only certified producers benefit
Divine Chocolate, coffee cooperatives

2.question: What was the Green Revolution and what were its main criticisms?
options: A 1960s–70s agricultural transformation using HYV seeds, fertilisers and irrigation that saved 1bn+ from starvation but increased inequality and depleted groundwater
A 1990s environmental movement that promoted organic farming and banned the use of chemical fertilisers in developing countries
A UN programme that distributed emergency food aid to sub-Saharan Africa during famines in the 1960s
A trade agreement that opened global food markets, reducing food prices for LICs but damaging small farmers
2.correctFeedback: Correct! The Green Revolution (1960s–70s) used HYV seeds, fertilisers and irrigation to transform yields in Asia and Latin America — saving over a billion people from starvation. But it also increased inequality (wealthy farmers benefited most) and depleted groundwater.
2.wrongFeedback: Not quite. The Green Revolution was a 1960s–70s agricultural transformation — Norman Borlaug's HYV seeds, fertilisers and irrigation dramatically boosted rice and wheat yields in Asia and Latin America, saving 1bn+ from starvation, but with costs to equality and the environment.

3.question: Almería in southern Spain is a case study for large-scale food production. Which of the following best describes it?
options: Over 30,000 hectares of greenhouses producing 3.5 million tonnes of vegetables per year, exported to northern Europe
A network of organic farms covering 3,000 hectares producing seasonal local produce for Spanish markets
A GM crop research centre producing drought-resistant tomatoes for export to sub-Saharan Africa
A government-funded agroforestry scheme combining olive trees with cereal crops across 30,000 hectares
3.correctFeedback: Perfect. Almería has 30,000+ hectares of greenhouse agriculture — the 'sea of plastic' visible from space — producing 3.5 million tonnes of fruit and vegetables per year for export to northern Europe. Year-round production, but with concerns about water use and labour conditions.
3.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Almería in southern Spain is famous for its 30,000+ hectares of greenhouses — visible from space as a 'sea of plastic' — producing 3.5 million tonnes of vegetables per year exported to northern Europe.

commonMistakes: Saying the Green Revolution 'solved hunger' — it dramatically reduced hunger in Asia but also caused inequality, groundwater depletion and pollution; always give both sides.
Confusing GM crops with the Green Revolution — they are different: Green Revolution used HYV seeds (natural selective breeding + irrigation); GM involves genetic engineering of DNA.
Forgetting that Almería has significant problems — it's not just a success story; high water use, migrant worker conditions and plastic pollution are important criticisms.
Treating 'sustainable food production' as just 'organic farming' — there are many other approaches: agroforestry, food waste reduction, urban farming, eating less meat, seasonal food.
Not giving specific examples for sustainable approaches — generic statements like 'reduce food miles' need a concrete example (e.g., Too Good To Go app, community supported agriculture).
examTips: For 9-mark food comparison questions: use the table structure — one paragraph on large-scale (with Green Revolution/Almería evidence), one on sustainable (with agroforestry/Fairtrade evidence), then evaluate which is more effective/sustainable.

Always evaluate the Green Revolution: 'While it saved over a billion people from starvation, it also increased inequality as small farmers couldn't afford seeds and fertilisers, and depleted groundwater through over-irrigation.'
UK food security questions often appear at the start of Section C — know the 60% self-sufficiency figure, the 45% vegetable import figure, and the impact of Brexit.
Fairtrade counts as both a sustainable food production strategy AND a development strategy — it bridges Topics G5 and G6 nicely.
revisionCardBullets: 690m chronically hungry; 2bn overweight — double burden of malnutrition
Causes: drought (Somalia 2011), flood (Bangladesh), soil degradation, pests, conflict, poverty
Locust swarms 2020 — destroyed crops in 10 East African countries
Green Revolution (1960s–70s) — HYV seeds, fertilisers, irrigation; saved 1bn+ from starvation
Green Revolution criticisms — inequality, groundwater depletion, chemical runoff
GM crops — herbicide-resistant, drought-resistant; EU bans; corporate control controversy
Almería, Spain — 30,000+ ha greenhouses; 3.5m tonnes vegetables/year; water + labour issues
Agroforestry — trees + crops; nitrogen-fixing, erosion prevention; West Africa
30% of global food is wasted — Too Good To Go, date label reform, behaviour change
Beef = 15,000 litres water per kg — eating less meat is high-impact individual action
UK: 60% self-sufficient; 45% of vegetables imported; Brexit disrupted supply chains
Russia-Ukraine war 2022 — fertiliser supply disruption; raised UK farming costs
